content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Help me pick a class movie

**Score:** 42
**Body:** I am a middle school teacher. Usually towards the end of the year, we get a chance to have a movie day in that liminal time between end of the marking period and the last day of school. I am a bit of a movie fan myself and like to go out of my way to show them something of quality that they might not otherwise seek out on their own. Last year I showed my students The Princess Bride. They loved it! I’ve even gotten requests to just run it back again this year.

However, I would like to show them something new. I am considering a Miyazaki film, but am looking for suggestions of any kind to gather ideas for this year (and beyond). My only request is that the movies avoid as much cursing and any adult/suggestive content as possible, preferably none. I can handle light cursing if it’s easy to track and mute in the moment. For example, I was able to mute/skip the lines in The Princess Bride when Inigo says “son of a bitch” and Wesley makes a comment about Buttercup’s breasts.

Thank you in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hugo (2011) i actually have memories of being shown this in school and i loved it, i’ve since rewatched it twice
2. Holes!!!
3. Willow! Other than being slightly terrifying for a child I think it should be appropriate!

4. HIdden Figures (2016) - STEM, civil rights, teamwork

School of Rock (2003) - Creativity, confidence, being yourself
  - School of rock is a great choice 
5. Kiki’s Delivery Service is perfect Miyazaki for middle schoolers. the protagonist is the same age, she’s graduated from witch school and is setting out to make a name for herself in the city, but she ends up in the gig economy as a flying parcel delivery courier. this causes her to burn out emotionally as she feels like she’s spinning her wheels in young adulthood, but overcomes her emotional problems with the help of her friends. and she saves the day when a zeppelin crash threatens the town. 
6. You can try back to the future, it's a classic (if there's any swears then I'm terribly sorry)
7. Hook … Fantastic Mr. Fox
8. The Iron Giant
9. Jumanji 

Beethoven 

Field of Dreams 

Flubber

Freaky Friday 

Adventures in Babysitting

Gremlins 

Harvey (this one is old, black and white, but it’s usually enjoyed by younger people) 

Babe

Homeward Bound

The Sandlot 

Little Rascals

Stuart Little

Savannah Smiles

Wild Hearts Can’t Be Broken

ETA: The Truman Show

## 2. Movies that are true works of art

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I recently watched The Fall (thanks to this sub) and I very much liked it. The movie is in my opinion not just a great movie, but a true work of art, both in terms of storytelling, characters, cinematography (filmed in 28 countries!) etc

Which other movies would you recommend that are this good?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Cell (2000).
2. A River Runs Through It
3. All of Guillermo del Toro's fantasy films, but especially Pan's Labyrinth 
4. Atonement
5. The Last Emperor
6. Ran.
7. Loving vincent :) 
8. A narrative-less documentary that was shot in 24 countries...Baraka (1992)

It is absolutely breath-taking.
9. Anything Neal Breen
10. Parasite

## 3. Suggest me the quintessential martial arts films

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I don't think I like this genre, but I don't think I've seen any of the ones that might define the genre or could change my mind. Through all history and all the world, which one would you recommend somebody start with?

EDIT: Just as an aside, how important is it that I watch with english dubbing vs subtitles. i typically much prefer subtitles to get the original performances, but if you tell me I'm missing out on something by not watching them the way they might have come on American TV in 70s, I'll give it a try.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Enter the Dragon
  - The Rolls Royce of martial arts films
  - I mean, enter the Dragon and The Big Boss are classics. The Shaw Brothers made many movies considered classics and Quentin Tarantino I'm sure has a lot to say about them.

But recent movies have received critical acclaim beyond cheesy directors. Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon was universally acclaimed and quite popular. Hero with Jet Li was amazing. I liked Curse of the Golden Flower, but my wife and son, less so. House of the Flying Daggers, brilliant!
2. The Raid (2011) is a favorite of mine. It has subtitles though.
3. I don't know if it's quintessential but I liked 'The 36th Chamber of Shaolin'.
4. Return of the dragon

Game of death

Fist of legend

Rumble in the Bronx 

Kung fu hustle

The postman fights back

Crouching tiger hidden dragon

Thats just what I could think of real quick. I know there's more, they just aren't coming to me. 

5. Check out the works of Jackie Chan particularly his 1970s through to the 90s films. His main genre was martial arts mixed with comedy. 
  - Drunken Master is one of my favorite Kung Fu movies 
6. Ip Man

Ong Bak 
7. There's American, Japanese and Chinese, and they all kick ass in their own way.

Start with something simple like Blood Sport or Kickboxer from the US, maybe Once Upon a Time in China from China (or any quality Jet Li film you can find from that era, they're all awesome), and something like Azumi or Ninja Assassin from Japan then figure out what you like.

## 4. The Thin Red Line

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Rewatched it. 

There is no better movie on war and its futility. 

I am speechless at how good this movie is.

The story. The acting the cinematography. The dialogues (“are you ever lonely?” …”only around people”). The script. The philosophical layers. 

Goodness. 

It’s not a movie. It’s a school of thought. 

Just watch it. Again. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just a warning. this is absolutely a love it or hate it movie. 

I think it's poetry on celluloid but I was just at a party a few weeks ago and literally walked in on a conversation about worst war movies and everyone agreed it was this. Well, except me. 

It's just not your typical war movie, or even your typical anti war movie. 


2. All it's missing are engaging characters and a plot.
3. It is one that I do hold in the highest regard. If I do think of one that might be 'better' i will let you know
4. Nick Nolte

"... God damn it when I said reinforcements you commit everybody you have there do it right now, everybody ...." 
  - His role was amazing in this movie. 
5. Just rewatched this last week, love it 
6. Terence Malick is a genius
7. I watched this in the theaters as a college freshman and it blew my mind.  But I’ve since seen it, and some other Mallick films, over the following decades, and it has never ever remotely hit me in the same way.

The Jisas Yu Holem Hand Blong Mi chant from the movie?  It’s so beautiful and I’ve listened to it so many times since.
8. Great soundtrack too.

## 5. I just broke my leg, give me your best 2026 releases so far.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Hey all, I was dumb enough to break my leg whilst mountainbiking and now I'll be homebound for a bit. I really wanted to catch up on some 2026 releases, as I've only seen a handful of them so far.

Ones that I've seen: Good Luck Have Fun Don't Die, which I quite loved, Project Hail Mary as well. The Drama I thought was alright and the less said about Return to Silent Hill the better.

I'd be especially interested in films you don't really hear much about, but are contenders for year end lists for you. Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Since you have a broken leg you should watch the 2026 release of 1954’s Rear window 

And then I’d say watch Wurthering heights with tom hardy 2009 in honor of what ever endless remake they made this year .


  - I second Rear Window! Disturbia was also enjoyable and Rear Window-esque. 

As far as 2026 movies, I was pleasantly surprised by How to Make a Killing. 
2. Send Help

3. Dead man’s wire. Fackham hall. Over your dead body. I swear. Bugonia. 
4. Roofman really surprised me
5. Nirvanna the Show the Band the Movie
6. Jury Duty is a good series 
7. misery

my left foot

rear window 

double indemnity
8. 28 years later: the bone temple 
9. Cold storage was aa surprisingly fun time 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are your thoughts on "The Patriot" (2000)?

**Score:** 694
**Body:** Curious what people's opinions are of this film.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jason Isaacs stole the show. Great performance.
  - Without Jason Isaac’s villian this movie would not have worked as well as it did.
    - You know, it's an ugly business doing one's duty. But just occasionally, it's a real pleasure.

      - Well, we're not going to hold him. We're going to *hang* him.
2. “Aim small, miss small” is a quote I use regularly, but not with respect to shooting people.
  - My rule for using a urinal.  
3. My dad worked on the film. He built the sets, furniture, and cannon hubs/wheels. The scene where heath ledgers character was shot and placed on a bed, my dad built. Fun fact, the bed was shortened to make the scene. If you look closely, heaths legs hang off the end.

Edit: He still has blue prints from the various things he worked on. Also he worked on Ace Venture Pet Detective 2, Under Siege, The Generals Daughter, Drop Zone, Holy Man (for a short time), Rosewood, and a few others. Also recently worked on a few TV shows in my hometown.
  - Did he build the chair that exploded in the beginning?
4. Every 4th of July this movie goes on. Jason Isaacs is incredible. The whole cast kills it.

![gif](giphy|7OX4NvySN2zJa5kIb9)


5. “My sons were better men”

## 2. Happy 45th birthday to Jessica Alba 🎂🎉. Liked her as Sue Storm in the 2000s Fantastic Four movies

**Score:** 373

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|1dZYJezwJmjfO)
  - Had a poster of her in this role hidden under my bed when I was a teen. 
2. Into the Blue
3. Dark Angel
4. Idle Hands and Never Been Kissed 💙
5. ![gif](giphy|xT9IgvOCwQTUSqwjGE|downsized)

This is the best Sue Storm, but Alba was amazing.
6. Sin city and never been kiss are my some of my favourites 
7. Wow i couldn't recognise her, she changed alot.
8. My first deposit in the spank bank was her in Dark Angel
9. honestly she *was* Sue Storm for an entire generation, those movies weren't perfect but she carried a lot of the charm and made the team feel believable

## 3. What's your favorite female comedic performance in a movie? [Scary Movie, 2000]

**Score:** 302

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Madeline Kahn in Blazing Saddles
  - Madeline Kahn in Clue


"Flames! F flames on the side of my face".
    - Madeline Kahn in anything!
2. Cindy! The tv’s leaking
  - This bitch is messing up my floor!
3. Anna Faris in pretty much anything, whether it's Scary Movie - 2 is my favourite, her and Chris Elliott are hilarious - The House Bunny, Smiley Face, heck she even made My Super Ex Girlfriend funny.
4. Shaky-spear!
  - I got you on camera, you on candid camera now! 😂
5. Either Julie Hagerty or Jive Lady in Airplane. They crack me up every time, I don't know how they kept straight faces 
6. Catherine O'Hara in Beetlejuice

## 4. Thoughts on Into the Wild? / Emile Hirsch

**Score:** 293
**Body:** I’m a huge fan of Emile Hirsch and his filmography! And Into the Wild was GREAT. I’ve been obsessed with him since I saw the trailer for The Girl Next Door.

What are your fave Emile Hirsch movies? Or.. what were your fave scenes in this movie?

Honorable mention- if you’re a fan of Matthew McConaughey & Thomas Haden Church - check out Killer Joe ! 🐓

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it's good, and the soundtrack is pretty strong. Vedder reading the phone book will have my interest. 

To me, it spoke to the meaning of the book that I took away. It's not a camping story, or a hiking story; it's basically about a kid who is clearly depressed and suffering from mental illness. I tried to commit suicide a few times, and something I learned about during those times in hospital stays was passive suicidal. I also thought that's what Chris dealt with. Sure, he fancied himself somewhat knowledgeable about camping and the wilderness, but I always saw it as a kid just trying to erase himself from the Earth on a slower timeline. 
  - The soundtrack alone makes it worth watching. Long nights is one of the best songs he’s ever done IMO. And I’m a Pearl Jam fan. 
2. If you can watch it as “just a movie” I think it’s pretty great. If you delve even a little bit in to the real story it falls apart pretty quick. 
  - Definitely. I loved the movie and afterwards read about what actually happened and the guy was a fucking idiot. Kinda disappointing 
  - You’re Wrong About did a great episode on Alexander McCandless that did almost ruin the movie. 
    - Christopher McCandless aka Alexander Supertramp.
3. Eddie Vedder did a great job with the Soundtrack.
4. It’s my personal favorite movie. I watched it for the first time when I was 19, in college, but aimless. This movie introduced me to new perspectives on life. Now I didn’t drop out of school to die in the woods. But it led me to find my love for camping, hiking and the great outdoors and inspired me to spend a few years working my way around the country that really helped me grow as a person. I graduated and got a job as a park ranger, spent a few years doing that, now 10 years later outdoor recreation has been my career and I love it. Helping others to have a safe and fun experience outdoors, wether it’s backpacking, campgrounds, or local parks is very rewarding to me
5. I liked the movie but it honestly I can't work out whether he was arrogant, or naive, or we're romanticising a mental breakdown.
  - I think all of the above tbh

## 5. Biggest missed opportunity?

**Score:** 207
**Body:** The Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare. Great cast, great subject matter, great director, a mediocre film. Even if you like the film, which is fine if you do, you have to admit it didn't reach the acclaim or popularity you would expect from the combination of the cast, subject and director it had.   

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zero tension the entire movie and the main villain was pointless.
  - Yeah it was an action movie on easy mode. 
2. Not about to pretend it was good, but I found it fairly entertaining. Found Cavill and Ritchson rather amusing in their roles. 
  - Also, loved the tiny glasses on Alan. 
3. I loved the throwback homage scene to Guns of the Navarone, the rest of the movie was kind of forgettable. Shame too because the production design was incredible. 
  - Action sequences were excellent.

If they removed the entire B-plot with the lady and the Nazi the movie would be the same.
4. I went to see this film with a couple of friends, both hated the film saying that they were expecting Inglorious Basterds and got disappointed. I was expecting King Arthur and got pleasantly surprised.

I'd argue that my expectation was more fair and grounded than theirs.
5. I remember watching it. I don't remember a single thing about it.
6. Yeah it was weak


7. I really wanted to like this movie but it was just so uninspired and boring. Which is too bad because the actors sure seemed to be having a good time.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. If you loved Possession (1981) consider watching Zulawsky's 1996 Szamanka (She-Shaman)

**Score:** 64
**Body:** It's not an easy film to find, at least it wasn't for me. Could not find a doable Bluray source, but did find this quite [low res YouTube of the full film](https://youtu.be/8ZQfoyf06jc?si=XclwJXtVQfump_jb) with English subtitles. I put off watching the film until I could find a good copy, and wasn't even sure I wanted to watch it given what I had read. Finally gave in and watched the YouTube, albeit on a big screen. Late in his film making career Zulawsky returns to Poland to make what reads as almost a meta-commentary film on Possession. The lead character is a sort of child-like saint/dolt of extreme sexual, and carnal desire that seems to comment on Isabelle Adjani's ecstatic, demon-loving murderess performance, taking what Adjani did and turning it inside out into something like a crypto female vampire story. So many of the frames and set ups in this film echo Possession, from the nuclear blast white-out to the female demonic saint of carnality. The lead male has many characteristics that Zulawsky used to parody a romantic rival in Heinrich, but this time played for less comedy, instead holding much of the films philosophical messaging. Saw it last night and still haven't indexed all the cross-commentary that feels embedded. Very graphic, very sexually driven (Cronenberg's Crash came out in the same year, with perhaps similar themes/effects). Also speculatively could not help but feel that Polanski's 1992 Bitter Moon is a target here, as well as pulling together other female tropes of super charged unreflective sexuality like in Betty Blue (Beatrice Dalle, 1986) and perhaps even influencing coming Besson films in The Fifth Element in 1997, and The Messenger in 1999.

For me Possession is a kind of masterpiece, nearly an unparalleled film in cinema. Szamanka's value and effect seems to come from how it reflects off of the earlier film. Almost a kind of extended, immense footnote to it. In that way its a very powerful film and is still resonating at really the philosophical and image level.

If you don't know or like Possession, not sure I would by default recommend it. Significantly over the top.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Very interested in checking this out. Love Possession so much.

Getting much more into foreign films this last year, it can be so disappointing how utterly inaccessible so many great films are. I’ve been trying to track down Obayashi’s filmography and it just feels so fruitless sometimes. Even with modern piracy it can be such a serious pain/borderline impossible. Possession is such an amazing and important film, it’s a shame that Zulawsky’s other work isn’t readily available to study.
  - I love horror films and I finally watched Possession. It was such a treat to be wowed by a horror film again. I love it as well. I am going to try and find this Szamanka.
  - Mondo vision has released dvds of a lot of his stuff, although they're special editions and so quite pricey: 37-45 euros
  - Zulawski's work is well available if you're willing to pirate. Szamanka doesn't have a bluray, only dvd rips. La Femme Publique got a bluray release just recently.

The Third Part of the Night, The Devil, That Most Important Thing: Love, Possession, La Femme Publique, L'Amour braque, On the Silver Globe, My Nights Are More Beautiful Than Your Days, Boris Godounov, The Blue Note and Cosmos all have blurays. Lot of them even with very good audio commentaries, with Zulawski's participation. I think only Szamanka and Fidelity don't have them. ​

Obayashi is trickier, but the major films also have good bluray rips.
2. If you loved Possession you should consider watching all other Zulawski films, he's such a singular director with a unique vision, that it's worth it. For me Possession is one of his weaker films, I like several of his more. 

I would say Szamanka is also one of his weaker ones. The Third Part of the Night and The Devil are equally out there, but more coherent. 
3. As a huge fan of “Possession” (whole catalogue of Żuławski is underrated in his home country Poland I’d say) Szamanka feels like first draft script that sticks mostly because of his craft and experience. But dialogue, composition doesn’t reach the intentionality and immersive dread as “Possession”. They have similarities thematic-wise but to me are kinda polar-opposites. With its character introduction, unsettling hate-love relationship “Szamanka” just lacks patience and rhythm with introduction of their traits and blooming relationship of two main characters. While “Posession” takes its time, but also introduces multiple themes simultaneously, coming back to them later. (Like with female teacher, lover of main characters wife etc.) 

If you liked “Posession” or “Szamanka” I’d highly recommend watching Żuławaki’s “Third part of the night”. It is kind of a horror, but a war-time one, apocalyptic as Żuławski himself put it. Written as partial reflection on war, stories of his Andrzej Żuławaki’s father during the war and some of his own. 
It’s also a psychological film. Go see it. 


  - Yeah, I hear you on \*Szamanka\* feeling almost like a raw draft compared to \*Possession\*. But honestly, that unhinged, almost messy energy kinda worked for me in its own way, like it was more immediate and less 'composed' dread.
4. Did you write this for me? I watched Possession last night (or tried to) but it was too much of a bummer after a recent breakup. I mean, it wasn't as messy as "Shoot the Moon," but I just wasn't in the headspace. I'll try going back in a few months and try it again.
5. I found this film after going through a bad break up with a schizoid typal girlfriend, and I found it spoke a lot to me.

I think it may be one of the first honest depictions of modern love and romance with a partner that is caught in a constant state of spiral and splitting.

Like a lot of Zulawski, it feels disturbing, unsettling, but there's an air of erotic escapsim throughout.

The soundtrack by Andrzej Korzynski is incredible.
6. Hell yes. Fantastic movie, definitely his maddest and most manic movie, which really says something.


It used to be my favorite of his (also the first one I watched), but his last movie, Cosmos, is by far my favorite now. I've seen it like 6-7 times and had to buy the book as well. Both the movie and the book are amazing. It's a great culmination of his work and imo his magnum opus. 

## 2. Stanley Kubrick’s interpretation of Holocaust representation in Schindler’s List. Do you agree with it?

**Score:** 58
**Body:** Allegedly, this is what Stanley Kubrick said about the film: “Think that's about the Holocaust? That was about success, wasn't it? The Holocaust is about 6 million people who get killed. Schindler's List is about 600 who don't" 

I will point out, the main character in Schindler’s List obviously wasn’t a victim of the Holocaust, he was part of the party and country that perpetuated it. However, the film does ultimately portray the various steps of how Germany committed it: Population displacement and segregation (Ghettos), state confiscation of property (Aryanization), forced labour, death squads (“Bullet Holocaust”), starvation, and gas chambers. This is how 6 million Jews were killed. 

I find Kubrick’s take interesting. Do you agree with him?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The context behind that reaction was that Kubrick spent years trying to develop a movie about the Holocaust, and ultimately gave up.  The Holocaust was simply too big and too horrifying to turn into a movie.

His closest attempt was “Aryan Papers”, an adaptation of the novel Wartime Lies.  A Jewish boy and his aunt get papers saying they‘re Catholic, so they survive the war, but in a way they’ve corrupted their own identity.  Which is also a story about the 2 who survived rather than the 6 million who didn’t.

So I don’t think Kubrick was actually criticizing Spielberg, really?  I think Kubrick was expressing his hard-won realization about the gulf between what you can do in a feature film, and his unfulfilled ambition to capture the true horror of the Holocaust.  Both Schindler‘s List and Aryan Papers only show a tiny corner of the full picture, but it’s easier for Kubrick to make that point referring to a Spielberg movie that exists, rather than his own shelved hypothetical project?
  - If I recall correctly Kubrick and Spielberg were in an ongoing communication about lots of stuff in the 90s including starting to collaborate on what became “AI” — apparently Kubrick mainly (only?) communicated by fax 😆 
But anyway I assume he felt ok about Spielberg given the relationship and how he basically told him he should be the one to make the AI film 
    - Kubrick talked on the phone a lot, too, having hours long marathon phone conversations with people at all hours of the night. Lol.

He and Spielberg first became friendly in the late 70s, and Kubrick invited Spielberg over for dinner with his family one evening when The Shining came out in 1980. Spielberg tells an interesting story about the conversation they had in one of his interviews.

Interestingly enough, when Kubrick approached Spielberg about directing A.I. in the mid-90s, with Kubrick producing, Spielberg *actually turned him down.*

He figured it was Kubrick's baby, since he'd been working on it for over 25 years at that point.
2. Kubrick planned to make his own Holocaust survival story with *The Aryan Papers*. He had even cast Joseph Mazzello in the lead after seeing him in *Jurassic Park*. However, after watching *Schindler’s List*, Kubrick abandoned the project, saying that essentially there was nothing he could contribute to the theme that Spielberg’s film hadn’t already done.

So I think it’s somewhat disingenuous to put a negative spin on Kubrick’s reaction to *Schindler’s List -* as it's often done. His response actually mirrors his approach to other abandoned projects, such as scrapping *Napoleon* even after years of intense research, partly influenced by the release of *Waterloo*. If Kubrick felt he couldn’t deliver the definitive contribution to a subject, he usually moved on to other projects.
  - "However, after watching *Schindler’s List*, Kubrick abandoned the project, saying that essentially there was nothing he could contribute to the theme that Spielberg’s film hadn’t already done."

Do you have a source for that? I don't think the exact reasons to abandon it where ever stated publicly. Pretty sure it was not because he though Schindler's was the final word on the topic. Also, we don't know if it would've been a "survival story", as Kubrick had not settled how to end it.

"Kubrick also references a ‘Kaputt’ ending, which refers to an ending that kills off all the major characters."

[https://www.sensesofcinema.com/2022/feature-articles/unrealisable-woman-tania-in-stanley-kubricks-aryan-papers/](https://www.sensesofcinema.com/2022/feature-articles/unrealisable-woman-tania-in-stanley-kubricks-aryan-papers/)
3. Kubrick also had a film about the Holocaust in development, *Aryan Papers*, and the release of *Schindler's List* was a major factor in him cancelling the whole project weeks before filming was supposed to begin. Kubrick was very concerned about the box-office, and had been slightly hurt by *Full Metal Jacket* being released after *Platoon* (due to delays caused by Robert Lee Ermey recovering from a car accident) and stealing some of its zeitgeist. According to his wife, another issue was that the matter was extremely bleak, and would put him in a depressed emotional state.

*Aryan Papers* is based on an autobiographical novel by Louis Begley, *Wartime Lies*, about a young Jewish boy and his aunt who pose as Catholics in Poland to escape persecution. The book doesn't show the camps, and is seen from the point of view of the kid, who doesn't fully understand what's happening (I don't know if Kubrick planned to film the "adult sections" of the book, in which the narrator, now an adult realizes how much these defining years resulted in his life being something of a fiction). In that regard, it's similar to The Pianist, another story that takes place during the Holocaust about someone merely trying to survive, with a wide tragedy being hinted at, not shown.

You have to remember that, before *Schindler's List*, there had been next to no narrative film directly about the Holocaust and the concentration and extermination camps. Dramatization of the Holocaust was regarded as taboo. Gillo Pontecorvo (*The Battle of Algiers*) had directed *Kapo* in 1960, its reception was mixed but it was excoriated by many critics, including the very influential *Cahiers du cinéma* in France, for being exploitative. The Holocaust was mostly covered by documentaries, such as Alain Resnais' *Night and Fog*, that showed the abandoned camps, or Jacques Lanzmann's *Shoah*. Plus of course, the 1978 *Holocaust* TV miniseries, which brought the term to popular usage. The mere use of dramatization was regarded as cheap regarding an horror beyond words. There could be a few scenes in a film dealing with the camps (the ending of Samuel Fuller's *The Big Red One* is haunting in that regard), but nothing more. Spielberg's justification for challenging this perception was that survivors wouldn't be there forever and that there was a need to preserve this story to future generations. But at release time, *Schindler's List* didn't receive universal acclaim.

Anyway, both Kubrick and Spielberg had understood that you couldn't center a film on the camps. Spielberg managed to spin it out as a somewhat uplifting story while not ignoring the gas chambers. That's something that Kubrick wouldn't have tried on his own. For Frederic Raphael, who's the source of this quote (in his memoir about working with Kubrick, *Eyes Wide Open*) and who's Jewish, just like Spielberg and Kubrick, it's an obvious dig at Spielberg, but beyond the quip I think that it's an acknowledgment of how you can't really make a narrative film directly about the mass extermination of the Jewish people from Europe, but need to approach it from a different angle, something that Spielberg had cracked and that Kubrick tried on his own a few years before making the comment, without being fully satisfied with the *Aryan Papers* project.
4. You could apply Kubrick's logic to Amistad (1997) and the parallel is almost one-to-one. Both films' focus is on a triumphant anomalous historical event, where good people with strong principles & morals become the saviours of the oppressed. Think about it, the defining characteristic of slavery in the Americas was inescapable generational bondage. Millions of people were stripped of their humanity, worked to death, and denied legal recourse. Amistad, however, focuses on a singular, historically anomalous event where a successful mutiny is followed by a successful legal defense (by a former US President no less) in the US Supreme Court in the 1840s. One could argue that Spielberg makes films about the triumph of the human spirit. He focuses on a pinprick of bright light amidst the darkest of abyss. And I feel there's merit in this perspective. I think Kubrick differs in that he wants the film to be as dark as the subject and he views the selected triumphant stories as self-deception of some sort. (Bear in mind I am a clueless person who wrote this on impulse)


5. Kubrick is my favorite filmmaker, but I don’t agree with him here:

— For one, Spielberg pays a price of entry for his optimism, in terms of how unflinching the film is.  The liquidation of the ghetto; the cruelty of Goeth, etc.  In the best sense, I don’t find the film compulsively watchable. I think Spielberg took himself to a place he was terrified of going.  

— Connected with this, I would have faulted Spielberg if he didn’t give us a sense of the magnitude of the horror outside the circle of his protagonists (Schindler, Stern, and those that are saved).  The girl in the red coat, the other victims, the billows of smoke in the camps:  he is not naive about it, nor sparing.

—Schindler, as a character, is a story of inner transformation: from his amoral indifference at the beginning  to a concern for others that had a positive impact.  Redemption narratives (“who cares; it’s just one guy”) are not a self-creation of Spielberg.  Dostoevsky’s “Crime and Punishment” is a redemption narrative, as is Shakespeare’s “King Lear”; Dickens’ “A Christmas Carol” and, even, “Great Expectations.”   The possibility of an inner transformation to an awareness of others’ pain (a theme of “King Lear”), and an ethic of care, has implications for the wider human community, even if one waves away the value of the inner transformation of a single person.  

— Connected with this, I thought the contrast between Goeth and Schindler moved the dial forward on a much contested link between cruelty and cowardice.  Part of the cowardice of Goeth is lacking the courage for self-knowledge and self-confrontation. He is a closed book to himself.  Schindler does the work of introspection. He’s strong, while Goeth is weak.  The philosopher Nietzsche threw down the gauntlet in asking the question “what is noble?”  He felt that Johann Wolfgang von Goethe was a specimen of nobility (Goethe had an impulse not of preying on those who were more vulnerable than he, but rather extending a hand to help raise them up).  Spielberg, through his script, goes beyond just saying “be good.”  He shows pusillanimity, small-mindedness, of cruelty and of the enlarged, soldier’s honor sense of aid to others that comes from strength. 


6. I had never read about Kubrick making the criticism, but it’s worth noting that this was not an unusual criticism of Schindler’s List at the time. Several Jewish intellectuals, including the filmmaker Claude Lanzmann, disliked the film because of its adherence to Hollywood narrative structure and melodrama, and also because they felt the focus on survival rather than destruction was  a mistruth.

i dont think that you can't make movies about the holocaust, but you shouldn't make them like this.

## 3. Do We Only Want “Safe” Women On Screen? Thoughts on Jennifer’s Body, The Bride!, Promising Young Woman, Carrie, Titane and Female Rage

**Score:** 51
**Body:** I keep thinking about how much movies shape what we think “women” look and act like. Most mainstream films still give us the same narrow templates: the good girl, the grieving or caregiving wife, the pretty muse who inspires the man, the cool girlfriend who softens him. Those characters can be powerful, but they quietly suggest that “real” women process pain quietly, stay reasonable, and keep their anger contained.

That’s why I’m drawn to films like Titane, Promising Young Woman, The Bride!, Jennifer’s Body, and Carrie. These movies center women whose emotions after harm don’t fit the “tragic sad victim” mold. Their protagonists are angry, contradictory, vengeful, selfdestructive, sometimes pathetic, sometimes terrifying. They don’t sit in tasteful grief. 

That’s not everyone’s experience, but it is some people’s. Someone I know who survived SA loved The Bride! because she didn’t turn into a quiet, dignified victim either; what she felt was an explosion of rage, disgust, numbness, then more rage. Watching the Bride cycle through those extremes felt, to her, like finally seeing her interior life on screen instead of yet another polished “sad but noble” survivor.

In contrast, I saw a thread titled something like: “I Have No Idea What The Bride! Is Trying to Say, But It Sure Is Loud About It.” That attitude is what I keep stumbling over. If a film is clearly about a facet of femininity you haven’t had to inhabit, unruly female rage, post‑trauma chaos, ambivalence about victimhood, why is the first move “it’s loud and saying nothing,” instead of “what is this expressing that I don’t immediately understand”?

At that point, it stops being just “this plot beat didn’t work” and becomes a question of whose inner life we take seriously. Do we only reward films where women stay within familiar, “respectable” emotional ranges? Or can we make space for stories where women are monstrous, petty, furious, contradictory, or nt remotely palatable?

I’m not saying you have to like any of these movies; they’re abrasive and not designed to be effortless watches. I’m asking: when you bump into a film that shows a version of womanhood you’re not used to seeing, do you treat that as an invitation to think, or just write it off as noise?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think that’s part of it. We’re getting better about allowing women in fiction to be nuanced and dangerous, but there’s still a hefty pushback. 

On top of that, audiences often react poorly to experimental fiction. *The Bride!* plays with structure and the fiction’s “reality” in ways that are uncomfortable by design, so it’s not surprising at all to me that it loses a large chunk of the audience on that front as well.

I personally am pretty conflicted in my opinions on it, but I’m so glad that movies like this are being made, to challenge both storytelling status quo as well as gender norm status quo.
2. I think this is an interesting idea - what do people connect to in cinema...  In general, men and women are taught (through books, films, and media) to see themselves in male characters.  But the reverse isn't true - boys aren't taught to empathize with the female perspective, and men even less so.  So I think the first big hurdle to connecting to angry women is that it's just not how we're typically socialized.  

The other big one is that the films you mention are made by women, and there just aren't that many women who have studio backing to fund their films.  You mention *Promising Young Woman*, I'll also go to bat for Fennell's *Wuthering Heights*.  Her Cathy is just a hot mess and I love her.  She's a brat and is petty and mean, but loves hard and is deeply passionate.  She's maybe a slightly different variation on the women you speak of.

The first thing I thought of when I read your essay is who are the women I connect to in film, and I would have to say that Greta Gerwig, and her ouvre, is who reflects my experience of womanhood.  It's not that I don't appreciate outward rage (I feel so much rage at the world these days) but she captures something that I deeply understand.  (The frustration and anger in her Jo and Marmee is the realest thing to me.)  

So getting back to your examples, I do think these films are finding their audiences (just based on Letterboxd logs alone), but they're not for everyone.  At the end of the day, abrasive cinema (or any media really) can be a bigger hurdle for most people because life is hard and sometimes it's easier to watch Below Deck.  

I do think things like social media is making it simpler for people to find and engage with female rage in film and TV.  *The Drama* is actually a great example of this - the movie is making bank and both Zendaya and Alana Haim play fully complex women who are messy and very human (and villainous at times). 

3. I don't think your point is entirely off-base and in many ways is correct about how oftentimes women in film are only allowed to inhabit certain spaces. I'm just not sure The Bride! is the best example of that, especially given that the other movies you named are all pretty well received. I'd also argue that at least in Carrie, she DOES fit the tragic sad victim role, it's just eventually she gets some form of retribution (but even with that doesn't have a particularly happy ending). Also, many of the movies you named are horror or horror adjacent and that's certainly a genre where you can see a range of roles for women.

To elaborate on your point though, women don't have nearly the same opportunity to be seen as a "default" character that white men do. It's often questioned if men will watch a female protagonist, whereas it seems rare to nonexistent when you see questions the opposite direction. Since a woman's place in a film is oftentimes seen more needing a justification, it's not surprising that you both see roles being more limited (to fit into expectations of the theoretical male viewer) and/or that certain members of the audience are less welcoming of roles outside of certain boundaries for women. 

All that said, I think the issue with The Bride! specifically is that it's more just kinda a mess of a movie.

I’m grouping them because they all center women whose responses to harm aren’t legible as dignified, tasteful, or ‘good victim’ behavior. The fact that The Bride! is being called loud, messy, and overdetermined is part of what interests me: why do we need women’s rage to be tidy and coherent before we consider it worth engaging with?

My point was that people calling The Bride! loud and messy has less to do with not wanting to engage with women's rage in moves and more to do with the movie being loud and messy.

## 4. Thoughts on 'Spotlight' (2015)?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Spotlight surprised everyone at the 2016 Oscars for Best Picture win over the likes of The Revenant, Mad Max Fury Road and The Big Short, despite only winning one other Oscar and having a mild lead up to the Oscars at the other ceremonies. 

However, few could argue about it's win. It is indeed a great film made very competently. The film is all about the screenplay and acting. Nothing flashy, in terms of cinematography or sound or editing. It keeps things simple filmmaking wise but all the genius is in the writing. It is expertly paced without getting into dramatical moments. The focus remains on journalism and the invesitigative work going on. The subject matter is of course very sensitive and they do a good job of walking a tight rope. 

The only complaint I had is that the film is directed in such a way that there is sometimes a lack of tension. For instance, I compare it with something like Zodiac which this film reminded me of, which also involved investigative journalism. But due to Fincher's style that film was always buzzing even if it has an anti-climactic ending. 

Spotlight felt very matter of fact and never reached the crescendo of something like a Zodiac. But still, it's a worthy winner although not as influential or iconic as its fellow nominee Fury Road.

Thoughts?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have a big soft spot for it. I like newsroom dramas done well and the stripped back style of this works wonderfully, but I also respect that it treats its audience like adults. Being matter of fact given the subject matter I think serves it better than trying to engineer something as dramatic or intriguing as Zodiac, but it is, IMO, equally horrifying. 

I always suspect that those who call it 'Oscar bait' have either forgotten it or haven't actually seen it. It's an unflashy film about incredibly sensitive, widely taboo subject matter that paints one of the most powerful institutions in the world as a corrupt and ghoulish organisation.
  - Incredible film and the pre-credits listing of *so many* fucked up parishes worldwide pushes me over the edge.
    - I remember being shocked at the first frame of the listing, and then it just went on and on and I was floored. 
2. There is a great line in the movie between Mark Ruffalo’s character and Stanley Tucci’s character when Stanley Tucci admits that he is Armenian and Mark Ruffalo says he is Portuguese. “This city, these people... making the rest of us feel like we don't belong, but they're no better than us. Look at how they treat their children.” That line always stuck with me.
3. It's pretty much a perfect film, in my opinion. Given the subject matter I wouldn't want anything different. I also love films about investigative journalism, so it's right up my alley.

I don't have much of an opinion on the Oscar win itself. I agree with Woody Allen when he said: "I don’t like the idea of awards for artistic things. **They’re not created for the purpose of competition**; they’re made to fulfill an artistic itch and hopefully entertain."
4. I honestly love Spotlight. Even though a difficult topic, I regularly catch myself wanting to revisit it. I love the performances, especially McAdams and Keaton and I just enjoy watching the film. I do have a weakness for films set in Boston though. I don’t know what it is about that city, but it’s always an interesting place. 

## 5. Magellan (2025) historical epic meets slow cinema

**Score:** 17
**Body:** The most recent project from Fillipino writer/director Lav Diaz, Magellan tells the story of Ferdinand Magellan (Gael Garcia Bernal) from the conquest of Malacca to his death in the Philippines during the first circumnavigation of the globe.

Its most salient feature is that it's **not** the spectacular historical epic you might imagine from that basic summary. It's very much slow cinema, with minimal camera movement and long takes. It's also a film about war and colonization with zero onscreen violence; we see the macabre aftermath of battles but not the battles themselves.

It's a fascinating, somewhat counterintuitive mix of style and subject matter that really worked for me, and I'm interested in other takes on it.

Of course I wouldn't recommend it to anyone but if you have an appreciation for photography then the slow, static compositions will not be a problem. Just watching how each shot was framed, how the composition changed when whatever action on screen took place, was enjoyable to me. And this is to say nothing of the themes/story.

There is nothing groundbreaking in the themes or story per se but where the film is groundbreaking is in its scope and attention to detail. Everything looks authentic, everyone feels authentic, to the point that it at times feels like a documentary. This is only enhanced by the fact that there is no nondiagetic music in the entire film. You really do feel as if you are experiencing these things as someone who was there would have experienced them.
